design_philosophy.core_loop: Explore locations, examine/interact with objects and NPCs, collect items, solve inventory-based puzzles to progress through story acts
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Puzzle solving and item discovery blocking narrative progression; balancing comedy with puzzle difficulty creates friction
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Experience the Discworld universe as Rincewind the wizard, interact with beloved characters, uncover conspiracies, and save the city through wit and resourcefulness
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Heavily prioritizes narrative, humor, and world fidelity over accessibility. Unapologetically British comedic tone with sophisticated voice acting. Celebrates difficulty puzzles as part of the authentic adventure experience rather than trying to streamline them. Designer Gregg Barnett explicitly focused on faithfully recreating novel elements, suggesting design choices favored lore coherence over player convenience.

difficulty.checkpointing: Chapter/act based progression with ability to revisit locations via overworld map
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: No explicit difficulty settings documented. Puzzle solutions are deterministic and non-optional for story progression. Limited inventory (4 items in pockets) creates resource management tension alongside puzzle-solving.

progression.upgrade_system: Item collection and knowledge acquisition (learning new conversation topics from discovered information)
progression.unlock_mechanism: Story-gated progression through four acts; new locations become accessible as players learn about them; L-Space access enables temporal exploration
replayability_hooks: Multiple conversation topics per NPC allowing different dialogue paths
Overworld map system enables exploration of discovered locations in player's chosen order
Rich character interactions reward multiple playthroughs for comedic depth

world_building_techniques: Point-and-click interaction with environment reveals lore organically
NPC dialogue trees with multiple conversation topics unlock contextual knowledge
Location-based storytelling through visual design and interactive objects
Meta-narrative: time loop mechanic (accessing L-Space to prevent book theft) creates recursive story structure
Character roster from source novels anchors world authenticity

boss_design.philosophy: Narrative-driven encounters serving story climax rather than mechanical skill tests. The dragon represents forces of chaos manipulated by conspiratorial brotherhood. Final confrontation depends on collecting 'hero components' (sword that goes 'ting', birthmark, magic spell) suggesting Pratchett's subversion of fantasy tropes.
notable_bosses: The Dragon (summoned antagonist) - defeated through preventing its summoning rather than direct combat
The Brotherhood (six members with individual roles) - defeated indirectly by acquiring their golden control items

faction_indicators: Secret Brotherhood (Chucky the Fool, Thief, Mason, Chimney Sweep, Fishmonger, Dunny King) - each represents a city social stratum
The Patrician - city ruler mediating between factions
danger_signals: Brotherhood's hidden control over urban institutions
Conspiracy threatening city stability masked as natural disaster
environmental_storytelling: Multiple locations across Ankh-Morpork and Discworld reveal social geography
Dragon lair stocked with gold suggests wealth concentration among conspiratorial elite
L-Space (magical library dimension) enables historical discovery of conspiracy origins
